coaching.coaching: Pin down non-vacuous signing proof in quiet Jira periods with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: non-vacuous signing proof in quiet Jira periods

coaching.coaching: Propagate the revision for canary workflow signing-secret materialize block to the child tickets.
coaching.subject: canary workflow signing-secret materialize block

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add guidance for the vacuous-run case: specify that if `rev-list "$C"..origin/tickets --count` returns 0 the operator should capture a new `$C` and re-trigger (or trigger a synthetic Jira event) until a non-vacuous run lands, so the signing proof is not indefinitely deferred.
evidence: The reconciler exits 0 (converged) with no writes when Jira has no changes to sync (exit-code contract at line 231–254 of reconcile-bridge.yml)
AC2(a) requires `git -C .tickets-tracker rev-list "$C"..origin/tickets --count` > 0 but the plan provides no fallback if this count is 0
The plan states 'wait for it to land ticket writes' but does not specify what to do if no writes land
findings.finding: The AC2(a) non-vacuous check (`rev-list "$C"..origin/tickets --count > 0`) can fail legitimately in a quiet Jira period when the reconciler converges with no new events to write, leaving signing unproven for that run. The plan has no mechanism to ensure or re-trigger a non-vacuous run, so the signing proof could be deferred indefinitely if the first triggered run is vacuous.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(a): 'non-vacuous — the run advanced the tickets branch with bot-authored commits'
scenarios: Operator triggers `workflow_dispatch` during a quiet period; reconciler converges cleanly with 0 new events; AC2(a) count check returns 0; signing is not proven; story cannot be attested.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What / Prove CI signs' section, add: 'If the triggered run is vacuous (count = 0, reconciler converged with no Jira changes), re-capture C = $(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD) and trigger again; repeat until a non-vacuous run lands. A quiet Jira project may require creating or updating a Jira ticket to force a write.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or verify) that setting `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` also activates signing in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml`, and confirm the canary's `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` default (`joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) matches identity 594c so both workflows sign consistently.
evidence: Plan Scope: 'The GitHub Actions workflow that writes tickets (reconcile-bridge). Secret set from SSM + one live signing proof.'
Plan Why: 'it reads the REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY secret' — attributed only to reconcile-bridge.yml
Actual: `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge-canary.yml:123` has `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY: ${{ secrets.REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY }}` with the same materialize-and-export logic
findings.finding: The plan scopes CI signing proof exclusively to `reconcile-bridge`, but `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` contains an identical `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` materialize block (lines 123-134) that will silently begin producing signed events once the secret is set. This is an unenumerated consumer of the secret-set action: the plan's AC1 says 'REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY on navapbc/rebar is set' with no acknowledgment that this simultaneously activates signing in the canary workflow.
scenarios: After the secret is set, any canary run will start signing events. If the canary writes an event whose signing fails (e.g., the key path resolves but the identity resolver can't match the canary's bot email to 594c), it surfaces as a signing-adjacent failure unrelated to the plan's stated proof run.
The operator-attested AC3 comment asks for evidence scoped to reconcile-bridge; a reviewer could reasonably question whether canary signing is also confirmed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Scope or Precondition section: 'Setting this secret also activates signing in `reconcile-bridge-canary.yml` (same materialize block); the canary's `BRIDGE_BOT_EMAIL` default is identical, so both workflows will resolve to 594c — no additional action required.' This documents the side-effect without expanding scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact AWS CLI retrieval command in step 1, e.g.: `aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text | gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar`
evidence: '`gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar` fed the decrypted SSM value (piped, never echoed)'
The exact retrieval command is absent; an executor must infer the AWS CLI invocation.
findings.finding: The plan describes piping the decrypted SSM value into `gh secret set` ('piped, never echoed') but does not state the exact AWS CLI retrieval command. The `--with-decryption` flag and `--query` / `--output text` options are required for a SecureString param, and omitting `--output text` would pipe JSON (including the key name) rather than the raw key material, silently corrupting the secret.
findings.location: What — step 1: 'Set the secret from the authoritative SSM source'
scenarios: Executor runs `aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` without `--with-decryption` or `--output text`, piping JSON or an encrypted blob into the secret — the run would then produce unsigned events and AC2b would fail with no obvious diagnosis.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the prose description with the literal pipe: `aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text | gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback statement for the behavioral change: e.g., 'To revert CI to unsigned/advisory mode, delete the secret (`gh secret delete REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY`) or set it to an empty value; the existing workflow secret-gate (`:220-222`) then resumes advisory output.'
evidence: "the 'rollback' for the write-only secret is simply to re-run `gh secret set` from the same SSM param" — this covers re-provisioning the correct key, not reverting to unsigned mode
No statement of the form 'to revert the behavioral change, set the secret to empty or delete it'
T12 requires 'an explicit, cheap, tested way to undo the change quickly'
findings.finding: The plan specifies rollback only for the 'wrong key written' case (re-run `gh secret set` from SSM) but does not provide an explicit rollback path for the behavioral change itself — reverting from signed CI events back to unsigned/advisory mode if needed after this story completes.
scenarios: A bug is discovered in the signing path post-provision; operators want to revert CI to unsigned/advisory mode while debugging but don't know the revert procedure
Enforcement story (8d39) is blocked and the team wants to temporarily disable signing without code changes
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line 'Rollback' note under the T4 parenthetical: 'To revert signed→unsigned behavior without touching SSM, run `gh secret delete REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar`; the workflow's secret-gated path resumes advisory (unsigned) mode immediately on the next run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the RETIRE/rotation path for `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY`: specify the trigger (e.g., SSM param rotation), the procedure (re-run `gh secret set` from the new SSM value), and who owns it — even a one-sentence reference to ADR-0012's rotation policy suffices.
evidence: Lifecycle covers CREATE ('Set the secret from the authoritative SSM source') and CONSUME (reconcile-bridge workflow) but has no RETIRE or key-rotation step
ADR-0012 is cited as the governance mechanism but no reference to how secret rotation is triggered or verified
T9 requires 'a RETIRE/cleanup path — it doesn't leak/accumulate'
findings.finding: The shared-state lifecycle for `REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY` omits a RETIRE/rotation path: there is no specified procedure for rotating or retiring the secret when the underlying ed25519 key pair is replaced or the bot identity is deprecated.
scenarios: The ed25519 key registered on identity 594c is rotated (e.g., compromise or expiry); the GitHub secret still holds the old private key; CI silently produces events that fail verification against the new public key
Bot identity 594c is deprecated; the secret persists indefinitely with no cleanup procedure

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] **T10/LEAST-PRIVILEGE IAM**: Specify the minimum IAM role or policy (e.g., `ssm:GetParameter` on `arn:aws:ssm:*:*:parameter/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` only) the operator must assume when running `aws ssm get-parameter --with-decryption` to retrieve the signing key, and record that role in the acceptance-criteria evidence comment.
evidence: Plan states 'fed the decrypted SSM value (piped, never echoed)' but names no IAM role, policy, or AWS authentication mechanism for the human operator retrieving the value.
ADR-0012 is cited as governing the SSM parameter but its IAM scoping details are not surfaced in this plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that a human operator retrieves the decrypted SSM SecureString value to set the GitHub secret, but does not state what AWS IAM identity or role that operator uses to perform the `ssm get-parameter --with-decryption` call. If the operator authenticates with broad/admin AWS credentials (e.g., PowerUserAccess or AdministratorAccess), this violates least-privilege for a sensitive key-retrieval action. The plan should name the assumed IAM role or policy that scopes the operator to only what is needed (SSM GetParameter on the specific path).
findings.location: What — Step 1 ('Set the secret from the authoritative SSM source') / Precondition section
scenarios: Operator authenticates to AWS with a broad role (e.g., AdministratorAccess) to retrieve the SSM SecureString; a least-privilege audit would flag this as excessive access for a one-time secret-set operation.
A future operator repeating this procedure copies the command without checking role scoping, normalising broad-credential use for sensitive key material.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-liner to Step 1: 'Operator must assume role `rebar-bot-secrets-reader` (or equivalent) scoped to `ssm:GetParameter` on `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` before running the `aws ssm get-parameter` command; verify with `aws sts get-caller-identity` before proceeding.' If no such scoped role exists, note it as a follow-up to ADR-0012.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add auth/precondition detail to What §1: specify the exact AWS CLI command (`aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text`), the required IAM role/profile the operator must assume, and the pipe form for `gh secret set` (e.g., `... | gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar --body -`).
evidence: Plan text: 'gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar fed the decrypted SSM value (piped, never echoed)'
No AWS CLI invocation, IAM role, or `--with-decryption` flag is specified anywhere in the plan
The SSM parameter is a SecureString (/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key) — decryption requires an explicit flag and appropriate KMS/IAM permissions
Sibling cache findings (.rebar/cache/plan-review/4dc0-d003-c236-45de/dfead84addcfcefe1ed5.json and c824509be8ea4158f2a1.json) independently flagged this same gap in a related plan
Evidence class: Partially-verified (SSM SecureString capability exists; the specific IAM/flag precondition for this operator context is unverified)
findings.finding: The plan's What §1 says the signing key is 'fed the decrypted SSM value (piped, never echoed)' but states no AWS CLI command, no required `--with-decryption` flag, and no IAM identity/role/profile precondition for the operator. This is a missing auth/precondition specification for the SSM retrieval step — a concrete operator action the story depends on to provision the secret correctly.
findings.location: What §1 / AC1 ('Set the secret from SSM')
scenarios: Operator lacks the required IAM permissions to decrypt the KMS-protected SecureString and retrieves an empty or encrypted-blob value, silently writing a broken key to the GitHub secret — the CI run then signs with a garbage key and verify-identity exits non-zero, failing the AC but leaving the cause ambiguous.
Operator uses `aws ssm get-parameter` without `--with-decryption`, receives the ciphertext blob, pipes it as the secret value — the workflow materializes a non-functional key file.
findings.suggested_fix:
In What §1, replace the prose description with the concrete shell pipeline:
```
aws ssm get-parameter \
  --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key \
  --with-decryption \
  --query Parameter.Value \
  --output text \
| gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar --body -
```
And add a precondition line: 'Operator must be authenticated as (or assume) the IAM role with `ssm:GetParameter` + `kms:Decrypt` on the `/rebar/prod/*` key path before running this command.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Prescribe the exact operator pipeline for Step 1 (e.g., `aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text | gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar --body -`) so the no-plaintext guarantee is enforced by command structure, not operator discipline.
evidence: Plan states 'gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar fed the decrypted SSM value (piped, never echoed)' without prescribing the exact pipeline

No canonical command (e.g., `aws ssm get-parameter --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text | gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --body -`) is given to enforce the piped-never-echoed guarantee
ABSENCE: the operator attestation AC records a timestamp but not the exact command used, so there is no post-hoc verifiability of how the key was transmitted
findings.finding: The SSM→GitHub pipe step (Step 1, 'Set the secret from the authoritative SSM source') asserts 'piped, never echoed' as intent but does not prescribe the exact shell invocation, leaving open operator patterns that could transiently expose the private key in plaintext — e.g., `--value "$(aws ssm get-parameter --with-decryption ...)"` exposes the key in the process table (`ps aux`), and storing it in an intermediate shell variable or file before piping writes it to shell history or disk.
findings.location: What / Step 1: 'Set the secret from the authoritative SSM source'
scenarios: Operator uses shell command substitution (`--value "$(...)"`) which makes the key visible to concurrent `ps aux` reads on a shared machine
Operator stores the decrypted value in a shell variable (`KEY=$(aws ...)`) which persists in shell history
Operator decrypts to a temp file then cats it, leaving plaintext on disk if the file is not immediately shredded
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Step 1 the canonical one-liner:
```
aws ssm get-parameter \
  --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key \
  --with-decryption \
  --query Parameter.Value \
  --output text \
  | gh secret set REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY --repo navapbc/rebar --body -
```
This pipes directly from SSM to `gh` without ever materialising the key in a variable, file, or argument string, satisfying `piped, never echoed` by construction. Optionally add `HISTFILE=/dev/null` prefix or run in a subshell with history disabled.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
